coredata.dc:description: Photo-Fenton-like catalysis allows development of novel advanced oxidation technology with promising application in wastewater treatment. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were intercalated between CuO nanoparticles and coralloid flower-like graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to fabricate a ternary CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid for synergetic visible-light-driven photo-Fenton-like oxidation. The CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid showed remarkable degradation efficiency towards recalcitrant organic contamination, excellent tolerance to realistic environmental conditions, exceptional stability and wide universality, declaring great potential for practical applications. •OH and •O2 − radicals were demonstrated to be the primary contributors in the photo-Fenton-like system. Mechanism studies reveal dual charge transfer pathways in the Z-scheme CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction assisted by interfacial electron transmission bridges of CDs, which can simultaneously boost the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ in the Fenton-like cycle and accelerate the Z-scheme electron flow from CuO to g-C3N4, leading to synergistic enhancement of the catalytic performance. This work would afford a feasible strategy to develop reinforced solar energy-assisted photo-Fenton-like catalysis systems for water remediation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo-Fenton-like catalysis allows development of novel advanced oxidation technology with promising application in wastewater treatment. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) were intercalated between CuO nanoparticles and coralloid flower-like graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to fabricate a ternary CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid for synergetic visible-light-driven photo-Fenton-like oxidation. The CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid showed remarkable degradation efficiency towards recalcitrant organic contamination, excellent tolerance to realistic environmental conditions, exceptional stability and wide universality, declaring great potential for practical applications. •OH and •O2 − radicals were demonstrated to be the primary contributors in the photo-Fenton-like system. Mechanism studies reveal dual charge transfer pathways in the Z-scheme CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction assisted by interfacial electron transmission bridges of CDs, which can simultaneously boost the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ in the Fenton-like cycle and accelerate the Z-scheme electron flow from CuO to g-C3N4, leading to synergistic enhancement of the catalytic performance. This work would afford a feasible strategy to develop reinforced solar energy-assisted photo-Fenton-like catalysis systems for water remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a continuing environmental and social problem, water pollution crisis has triggered considerable attempts to develop efficient water purification technologies. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) are appealing options owing to the advantage of generating high-oxidation reactive oxygen species to completely destroy organic pollutants in water [1]. Among them, homogeneous Fenton reaction, which induces the production of extremely reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH) with the existence of Fe2+/H2O2, has been utilized as an effective water treatment technology due to its high efficiency, low cost, and simplicity [2,3]. Nevertheless, some shortcomings impede its extensive applications, such as low H2O2 utilization rate, strict pH range (2.5–3.5), and excess generated ferric hydroxide sludge causing secondary pollution [4]. Developing hetergenous Fenton-like system is one of the hotspots to overcome the above drawbacks. Various Fe-based materials such as FeOOH, Fe2O3, and Fe3O4 have been reported as hetergenous Fenton-like catalysts for pollutant removal [5,6].

Compared with traditional Fe-based catalysts, Cu-based materials with similar redox properties have been regarded as inspiring alternatives on account of higher catalytic efficiency of the Cu2+/H2O2 system and wider pH range of 3∼14 [7–9]. In particular, copper oxide (CuO) with enormous abundance, low cost, ease of manufacture, and environmental friendliness attracts much attentions, which can provide Cu2+/Cu+ Fenton cycle and absorb visible light energy [10]. For example, Xiao et al. synthesized CuO nanosheets though a hydrothermal method, which was combined with trace ascorbic acid to accelerate the redox cycles of different valence states of copper, exhibiting excellent Fenton-like catalytic activity [11]. Fang et al. prepared CuO nanosheets from simulated Cu2+ waste effluents by alkaline H2O2 reaction, which formed self-cyclic Fenton-like reaction to achieve complete degradation of phenol within 40 mins under visible light irradiation [12]. Nevertheless, the sluggish Cu2+/Cu+ cycle efficiency and unsatisfactory inferior recyclability still blocks further development and applications [13]. It is urgently needed to develop reinforced heterogeneous Cu-based Fenton-like catalysts for wastewater remediation.

As an environmental-friendly AOP for contaminant elimination, photocatalytic degradation technology has also gain intensive attention in recent years. Particularly, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been regarded as a fascinating metal-free photocatalyst owing to the easy preparation, suitable band gap of 2.70 eV, visible-light response ability, and adjustable electronic structure [14,15]. However, severe electron-hole recombination and limited oxidation capacity of photo-generated holes on valence band seriously restricts the catalytic performance [16]. To solve these problems, artificial Z-scheme heterojunction systems have been developed by mimicking natural Z-scheme photo-synthesis, in which the photoinduced charge carriers not only retain strong both reduction and oxidation abilities, but also boost the isolated photo-generated e− and h+ to effectively reduce their recombination rates. For instance, Mohamed et al. reported a series of metal oxides/g-C3N4-based Z-scheme systems for efficient photocatalytic Hg2+ reduction, suggesting that the superior Z-scheme heterostructure can dramatically enhance the separation rate of electron-hole pairs [17].

Given their respective features, combining photocatalysis with Fenton-like catalysis to construct a heterogeneous photo-Fenton-like system provides a prospective synergetic AOP [6,18]. Remarkably, Cu-based oxide materials and g-C3N4-based composites have been attracted much attention to fabricate photo-Fenton-like catalysts. For instance, Zhong et al. grew Cu2O on MIL100(Fe) metal–organic frameworks to obtain a Cu2O/MIL(Fe/Cu) composite via an in-situ Cu-bridging strategy for enhancing the interface interaction, which exhibited fast kinetics and high mineralization for thiacloprid degradation [19]. Kirti Sharma et al. synthesized CuO/g-C3N4 via a thermal calcination method, which was coupled with H2O2 to build a photo-Fenton-like system, resulting in higher 2, 4‑dimethyl phenol (DMP) degradation efficiency (99 %) under visible light irradiation [20]. To integrate photocatalysis with Fenton-like catalysis processes, rapid transmission of the photo-generated electrons is of great significance. However, how to construct efficient charge transfer pathways between heterogeneous interfaces is still a grand challenge. Given the capability of harnessing long wavelength light and high conductivity, zero-dimensional (0D) carbon dots (CDs) with sp2 carbon inserted by sp3 hybridized carbon serve as a cogent electron-transfer constituent for photocatalysts or photo-Fenton catalysts [5,21,22]. Therefore, CDs would provide a promising interfacial linkage for developing efficient photo-Fenton-like catalysts.

Herein, we rationally introduce 0D CDs into Z-scheme CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction to construct a ternary CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid as a synergetic visible-light-driven Fenton-like catalyst for wastewater treatment. Through building dual charge transfer pathways assisted by interfacial electron transmission bridges of CDs, the CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid can simultaneously boost the Fenton-like reaction and photocatalytic processes, so as to implement a reinforced photo-Fenton-like system. To validate the potential for practical applications, the catalytic performances of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid were systematically evaluated, including the stability, universality for organic pollutant elimination, initial pH values, H2O2 dosage, and the influence of various realistic water matrices. The multiple electron transfer pathways and possible mechanism for the synergetic photo-Fenton-like oxidation processes were also proposed. This work aims to develop synergetic photo-Fenton-like catalysts via strengthening interfacial charge transfer for efficient wastewater treatment.

According to Scheme 1 , the CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid was prepared via a facile liquid deposition approach. A certain carbon dots (CDs, 0.75 mg) was added into 20 mL g-C3N4 suspension (1.67 mg mL−1). Since ultrasonication and agitation for 2 h, the attained uniform mixture was transported to the 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and kept at 120 °C for 2 h. After cooling naturally to ambient temperature, the impurities were removed from the mixture though filtration and washing with deionized water for several times. The resulting mixture was dispersed in 86.3 mL copper sulfate solution (CuSO4, 40 mg L−1), of which the pH value was regulated to 12 with sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH, 1 mol/L). Furthermore, 435 μL hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) was added into the obtained mixture under vigorous stirring for 20 mins. The sample was acquired after washing with deionized water and vacuum drying at 60 °C for one night, marked as CuO/CDs/g-C3N4. To evaluate the effect of CDs, the weight percentages of CDs relative to g-C3N4 were modulated as 0.5 %, 1.5 %, 3 %, 5 % to obtain CuO/x% CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids (x = 0.5, 1.5, 3, 5). As controls, CuO/g-C3N4 were prepared with the similar method without adding CDs. Pure CuO was synthesized without adding g-C3N4 and CDs [12]. CuO/CDs/bulk g-C3N4 was obtained by replacing the coralloid flower-like g-C3N4 with bulk g-C3N4. Detailed information of the used reagents and preparation methods of CDs and g-C3N4 are depicted in the Supplementary Material.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30, FEI), X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical X-ray diffractometer), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermol scientific Escalab 250Xi spectrometer) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet 6700 spectrometer) spectra were employed to investigate the morphology, structure and component of the samples. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas were investigated by nitrogen adsorption/desorption analysis (BELSORP MAX). UV–vis. diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis. DRS, Shimadzu UV-2700) and photoluminescence (PL, F-97 Pro, Technology) spectra were measured to evaluate optical properties of different samples. Transient photocurrent responses (TPR), electrochemical impedance (EIS) and Mott-Schottky measurements (M−S) were conducted on a CHI 760e workstation to investigate the electron transfer properties and the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) positions of different samples. Electron spin resonance (ESR, JES-X320) spectra were conducted to clarify the main reactive species in the photo-Fenton-like system. Detailed operation procedures are listed in the Supplementary Material.

To evaluate the visible-light-driven photo-Fenton-like performance, methylene blue (MB, 20 mg/L) was selected as a simulated contaminant, while the catalyst dose was determined as 0.2 g/L. Additionally, different reaction conditions were conducted to explore practical application potential of the catalyst, including different pH values, different contaminants [including tetracycline (TC), rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO), and p-nitrophenol (p-NP)], different anions, different realistic water environment matrix and different humic acid (HA) concentrations. To test the reusability, the catalyst was recovered through filtration and reused in sequential cycles. Radical and hole capturing experiments were conducted by adding additional quenching agents in the reaction system. Detailed operating methods are listed in the Supplementary Material.

The morphologies and microstructures of different catalysts were monitored by SEM and TEM images. Compared with bulk g-C3N4 displayed in Fig. S1, SEM image of g-C3N4 exhibits a coralloid flower-like structure with folded lamellas (Fig. 1 a), and CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 maintains the initial morphology of g-C3N4 (Fig. 1b). TEM image of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 confirms the lamellar structure with abundant folded and interlaced wrinkles (Fig. 1c). Enlarged HR-TEM images of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 reveal the existence of CDs (marked with red cycles) with an average size of 2.5 nm and CuO nanoparticles anchored on g-C3N4 nanosheet matrix, which can be verified by the lattice distance of 0.334 nm and 0.232 nm corresponding to (002) plane of CDs and (111) plane of CuO, respectively (Fig. 1d, e) [23]. Apparently, different from the leaf-shaped microstructure of pure CuO (Fig. S2), the CuO nanoparticles are assembled by multi small nanocrystals in CuO/CDs/g-C3N4. As shown in Fig. 1f-g, dark-field TEM images and corresponding elemental mappings clearly display the uniform distribution of C and N elements across the whole substrate, with CuO aggregates anchored on g-C3N4. Furthermore, N2 adsorption/desorption analysis was examined to investigate the BET surface area of the samples. As shown in Fig. S3, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms are classified as type IV isotherms for both g-C3N4 and CuO/CDs/g-C3N4. Generally, bulk g-C3N4 contains poor specific surface areas (as low as ∼ 10 m2/g), which has been demonstrated in our previous research and related literatures [24–26]. Compared with bulk g-C3N4, the coralloid flower-like g-C3N4 exhibits an increased specific surface area of 94.31 m2/g, which provides an ideal platform for hybridization with CuO. Precisely because of the lamellar structure, CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 maintains a similar specific surface area of 93.48 m2/g after the introduction of CuO and CDs. The composite structure and high surface area of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 would provide more adsorption and active reaction sites for subsequent catalytic degradation process.

XRD patterns and FTIR spectra were conducted to confirm the composition of the samples, as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S4. XRD pattern of g-C3N4 shows the characteristic (001) and (002) planes at 12.9° and 27.7°, which are attributed to the interplanar packing and graphitic layer stacking of π-conjugated sheets, respectively (Fig. 2a) [27].The diffraction peaks in pure CuO at 32.5°, 35.5°, 38.7°, 48.7°, 58.3°, 61.5°, 66.25°, and 68.1° are indexed to (−110), (002), (111), (−202), (202), (−113), (−311), and (−220) planes, respectively, in well accordance with tenorite (PDF#45–0937) [28]. When combining CuO with g-C3N4 and CDs, the CuO characteristic peaks can be observed in CuO/g-C3N4 and CuO/CDs/g-C3N4, while the (002) plane of g-C3N4 shifts to a lower 2θ position of 27.5° and 27.4°, which should be attributed to partial infusion of CuO into g-C3N4 interlayers and introduction of CDs [29]. Characteristic diffraction peaks of CuO and g-C3N4 can all be observed in the XRD patterns of CuO/x%CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids with different CDs loadings (Fig. S4a), indicating that CDs have negligible effect on the formation of the heterostructure. Moreover, FTIR spectrum of CuO exhibits sharp peaks at 512 and 612 cm−1 ascribed to the strething vibration of Cu–O (Fig. 2b) [30]. All tpical absorption peaks can be observed in FT-IR spectrum of g-C3N4 [31]. As a result of overlaying with the strong absorbed peaks from g-C3N4, characteristic bands of CDs cannot be observed in the FTIR spectra of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrids (Fig. S4b). While typical absorption peaks of CuO and g-C3N4 are shown in FTIR spectra of CuO/g-C3N4 and CuO/CDs/g-C3N4, indicating the coexistence of the two components.

The surface component and chemical state of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 was further examined by XPS measurements. As shown in Fig. S5, the full range XPS spectrum shows the presence of C, N, O, and Cu signals in CuO/CDs/g-C3N4. High-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum can be deconvolved into four peaks ascribed to CC (sp2), CO/C-NH2, CNC, and sp2-hybridized carbon in NC-N aromatic rings, which are centered at 284.6, 286.5, 287.8, and 288.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 3
a) [32,33]. Four characteristic N 1 s deconvoluted peaks of g-C3N4 can be observed, including CN-C, N-(C3), CNH, and π excitation peaks, which are located at 398.6, 399.9, 401.1, and 404.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b) [34,35]. As to the O 1 s XPS spectrum, the peaks at 529.6 and 533.1 eV belong to Cu–O and COCu [36], respectively, while the CO and C-OH/COC peaks are located at 532.1 and 532.5 eV arising from the surface oxygen-containing groups of CDs (Fig. 3c) [37,38]. Fig. 3d presents the Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 bands centered at 932.9 and 952.8 eV, respectively, with satellite peaks at 941.5, 943.7, and 962.2 eV correlating to the d9 configuration of Cu2+ [29]. Remarkably, the main fitted peak at 933.5 eV further identifies the existence of Cu2+ species [39], while the 932.5 eV peak assigned to Cu+ indicates partial reduction state of CuO [7]. These results indicate the co-deposition of CDs and CuO on g-C3N4 matrix to construct the ternary CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid.

To gain insight into the optical properties of different samples, UV–vis. DRS and PL spectra were measured. The visible light absorption edge of g-C3N4 is situated at about 449 nm, and CuO possesses a wider visible light absorption region (Fig. 3e). CuO/g-C3N4 merges the light absorption regions of g-C3N4 and CuO. Notably, broadened absorption region and promoted visible light absorption can be achieved by the intercalation of CDs. This is further supported by UV–vis. DRS of CuO/x% CDs/g-C3N4 with different CDs contents, among which CuO/1.5 %CDs/g-C3N4 stands out (Fig. S6a). PL spectra reflects the recombinant rate of the photoinduced charges. Owing to low response to light, pure CuO exhibits a small PL signal, whereas g-C3N4 shows the highest PL signal intensity among the samples, indicating the high electron-hole recombination rate of pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 3f). Through hybridization with CuO, CuO/g-C3N4 presents a dramatically reduced PL signal, suggesting that a Z-scheme heterojunction structure has been successfully formed. Interestingly, the CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid emerges the weakest PL signal, implying that intercalation of CDs can efficiently boost the electron transfer and restrain the recombination of photoinduced electron-holes (Fig. S6b). These versatile optical features with excellent visible light harvesting capacity and promoted electron transport efficiency render CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 great potential for application in visible-light-driven photo-Fenton-like catalysis.

The visible-light-driven photo-Fenton-like oxidation performance of the samples was firstly evaluated by degradation of MB as a simulated organic pollutant in water. As shown in Fig. 4a, after dark adsorption equilibrium, pure g-C3N4 and CuO exhibit sluggish MB degradition curves under visible light irradiation, suggesting their unsatisfactory photo-Fenton-like activities. By contrast, CuO/g-C3N4 presents an impoved catalytic efficiency on account of the heterojunction structure. Tellingly, by intercalating 0D CDs between the CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction, the ternary CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 delivers distinctly boosting photo-Fenton-like catalytic performance, achieving a superior removal efficiency of 100 % within 40 mins. Accordingly, as listed in Fig. 4b, CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 displays the highest reaction rate constant of 0.1980 min−1, which is nearly 11.79, 5.86, and 2.35 times higher than that of g-C3N4, CuO, and CuO/g-C3N4, respectively. When replacing substrate with bulk g-C3N4, the CuO/CDs/bulk g-C3N4 exhibits a depressed catalytic efficiency with the reaction rate constant of 0.0575 min−1, revealing the signicifant role of coralloid flower-like g-C3N4 for enhanced light absorption and homogenous distribution of CuO. As a control, the mixture of CuO, CDs, and g-C3N4 gives the similar rate constant of 0.0524 min−1, which indicates that intimate interfacial connections between the components are crucial for a synergetic photo-Fenton-like system. To investigate the effect of CDs contents on photo-Fenton-like catalytic performance, MB degradiation over CuO/x%CDs/g-C3N4 were evaluated under visible light illumination, as shown in Fig. 4c. All the hybrids afford desirable degradation rates, and CuO/1.5 %CDs/g-C3N4 exbihits the best peformance among all the samples. Therefore, moderate CDs could effectively reinforce the photo-Fenton-like process, while excess CDs would shield light adsorption and active sites, resulting in slightly dropped catalytic activities.

Efficiently integrating photocatalysis with Fenton-like reaction over CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 is futher proved in Fig. 4d. The poor elimation efficiency in H2O2 and visible light system without CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 indicates the vital role of catalyst. CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 exhibits a slight elimination of MB without H2O2, whereas it shows a moderate catalytic efficiency in the absence of visible light, suggesting the modest Fenton-like activity. Apparently, the hybrid exhibits outstanding performance with the appearance of both H2O2 and visible light, illustrating the cooperative photo-Fenton-like system. The reusability and general applicability of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 was further evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 4e, CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 retains a preferable catalytic efficiency with consecutive six catalytic cycles, revealing the prominent stability of the catalyst. Furthermore, the ternary hybrid exhibits well degradation performance for eliminating various pollutants including MO, RhB, p-NP, MB, and TC, thereby confirming its high efficiency and excellent universality (Fig. 4f).

As vital reaction factors, the influence of initial pH value, H2O2 dosage and water matrix were further explored to evaluate the practical application potential of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid. As shown in Fig. 5 a, CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 exhibits excellent catalytic activities in a wide pH (5 ∼ 10), which affords the highest efficiency at pH = 10. This should be mainly ascribed to the formation of copper hydroxide in alkaline conditions, which has been regarded as one of the best Fenton-reactive species [40,41]. The decreased activity at pH = 3 might be assigned to the etching of CuO in the acid environment. Fig. 5b reveals that MB catalytic elimination efficiency increases with increasing H2O2 dosages. Remarkably, the removal efficiency can reach to 100 % in 50 mins with a low dosage of 5 mM H2O2.

To ascertain the application potential in realistic environment, MB elimination performances in various realistic water environmental matrices involving deionized water, sea water, river water, wastewater, and underground water were methodically investigated (Fig. 5c). It is worth mentioning that the CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 displays prominent catalytic performances and gives elimination efficiencies of 100 % within 40 mins in all water matrices. The elimination of MB can also be denoted though a pseudo-first order kinetics with high reaction rate constants (Fig. S7a and Fig. 5d). Natural organic matters and inorganic anions in water would affect the actual application performance of Fenton-like catalysts. Thus, as one of representative organic matters, humic acid (HA) was selected to discern the effect of natural organic matters (NOMs). As described in Fig. 5e and Fig. S7b-c, the MB elimination efficiency decreases from 99 % to 90 % within 20 mins with the increasing HA concentration from 0 to 20 mg/L, revealing slightly negative effect of HA on the catalytic activity. Additionally, the effect of inorganic anions including Cl-, NO3 –, PO4 3-, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, as well as HCO3 – with different concentrations was further investigated. It is found that most inorganic anion ions show negligible effect on the elimination efficiencies except for Cl- (Fig. 5f and Fig. S8). The depressed activity resulted from Cl- should be ascribed to its capacity to scavenge •OH [42]. Notably, the PO4 3-, CO3 2–, and HCO3 – ions can even lead to promoted elimination rates, which is possibly attributed to the increased pH induced by these anions, in accordance with the effect of pH value on the catalytic performance. Considering the above results and water quality parameters of various water matrices listed in Table S1, it can be deduced that the CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 exhibits satisfactory performance under realistic water matrices and emerges outstanding tolerance with natural organic matters and inorganic anions, illuminating great practical application potential in water remediation.

TPR and EIS photoelectrochemical measurements of CuO/g-C3N4 and CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 were compared for clarifying the role of CDs in the catalyst. Fig. 6a shows the TPR results under intermittent visible light illumination for several on–off cycles. Compared with CuO/g-C3N4, a higher photocurrent density of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 is observed when the light is switched on, indicating that the introduction of CDs is conducive to photo-generated electron transfer between heterogeneous interfaces. Remarkably, as described in the EIS results, the smaller semicircle at the low frequency region and higher slope at the high-medium frequency region of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 than CuO/g-C3N4 provides direct proof for enhanced electron transmission ability induced by CDs (Fig. 6b). What’s more, EIS measurements of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 and CuO/CDs/bulk g-C3N4 were also implemented to testify the influence of the g-C3N4 substrate (Fig. S9). Compared with CuO/CDs/bulk g-C3N4, the smaller semicircle and higher slope indicate that the coralloid flower-like structure of g-C3N4 endows CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 much lower charge transfer resistance and higher matter diffusion kinetics [43].

Impact of CDs on the production of reactive •OH species was further investigated. Based on a coumarin fluorescent probe method, the •OH concentration was monitored through typical fluorescence peak intensity of 7-hydroxycoumarin (7HC) at around 453 nm formed in the reaction of •OH and coumarin (COU) [44]. The increasing fluorescence intensity with reaction time certifies the continuous formation of •OH in both CuO/g-C3N4 and CuO/CDs/g-C3N4-based photo-Fenton-like systems (Fig. 6 c). While the higher fluorescence intensity of CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 demonstrates the boosted generation rate of •OH induced by the introduction of CDs. To shed light on the enhancement mechanism, high-resolution Cu 2p XPS spectra of fresh and recycled CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 were compared (Fig. 6d). After the photo-Fenton-like reaction, the atomic ratio of Cu+/Cu2+ in recycled CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 increases from 0.65 to 2.09, suggesting the smooth conversion between Cu2+ and Cu+ in Fenton-like cycle [45]. The above results manifest the crucial role of CDs in building efficient interfacial electron transmission bridges to enhance the Cu+/Cu2+ redox kinetics, accounting for the reinforced photo-Fenton-like performance.

Determining the dominant reactive species is the key to elucidate the reaction mechanism, so in-situ ESR analysis and radical trapping experiments were carried out. The •OH and •O2 − radicals were identified by employing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolidine-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping agent. As described in Fig. 7 a-b, there are no characteristic signal peaks in dark, suggesting that •OH and •O2 − are not generated. After visible light illumination for 5 mins, strong characteristic signals of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − can be observed, which become stronger when prolonged to 10 mins, declaring the continuous generation of •OH and •O2 − in the photo-Fenton-like system [46]. Additionally, TEMPO was utilized as a spin-trapping agent to monitor the generation of photo-generated holes (h+). The signals of TEMPO-h+ obviously weaken with increasing light irradiation time (Fig. 7c), which suggests that photo-generated h+ are continuously generated under visible light irradiation [47]. Moreover, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), trichloromethane (Tri.), and disodium ethylenediamine acetate (EDTA-2Na) were utilized to trap •OH, •O2 −, and h+, respectively. The role of O2 was discerned though control experiment of purging N2 to remove O2 (Fig. 7d). Obvious inhibiting effect of different scavengers can be seen as following the order of IPA > Tri. > EDTA-2Na, illuminating that the generated •OH, •O2 − and h+ serve as major reactive species in the pollutant elimination system. Among them, •OH and •O2 − are considered to be the primary contributors. Besides, the markedly declined degradation efficiency under N2 atmosphere further reveals the significant role of generated •O2 − radicals derived from O2 in the photo-Fenton-like oxidation process.

Considering the semiconductor characteristics of g-C3N4 and CuO, the band gaps were obtained though plotted curves of (αhν)2 or (αhν)1/2 vs photon energy (Fig. S10 a-b), and the position of conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) were attained through Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. S10 c-d). Accordingly, g-C3N4 and CuO are confirmed as a n-type and p-type semiconductor with band gap of 2.48 and 1.95 eV, respectively. The ECB of g-C3N4 and CuO are calculated to −1.01 and 0.17 V, respectively, while the EVB are calculated to be 1.47 and 2.12 V (

, details in the Supplementary Material). According to the CB and VB positions, possible charge transfer pathways in the photo-Fenton-like catalyst can be speculated, as shown in Fig. 7e. Due to the more positive CB potential of CuO than E0 (O2/•O2 −) (−0.046 V vs. NHE) [48] and E0 (Cu2+/Cu+) (0.153 V vs. NHE) [49], the photo-generated electrons (e−) accumulated in CB of CuO could hardly transform O2 and Cu2+ into •O2 − and Cu+, respectively. Then a conventional CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction illustrated in hypothesis A can be excluded. If the electrons (e−) in CB of CuO could be directly transferred to the VB of g-C3N4 to form a Z-scheme heterojunction, the e− in CB of CuO could be transferred to VB of g-C3N4. While the e− accumulated in CB of g-C3N4 can transform O2 into •O2 − due to the more negative potential than E0 (O2/•O2 −). Thus, a Z-scheme heterojunction with an electron transmission mediator of CDs bridging the two semiconductors can be inferred (hypothesis B-D). Unfortunately, as described in hypothesis B, the redox of Cu2+/Cu+ would not proceed by employing the e− in the CB of CuO for its more positive potential. Considering the smooth Fenton-like reaction over CuO/CDs/g-C3N4, it is more likely that the e− in the CB of g-C3N4 could be transferred and accelerate reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ with assistance of CDs bridges. Therefore, dual charge transfer pathways can be speculated owing to the prominent electron-transfer ability of CDs verified by the above characterizations, as illustrated in hypothesis C-D. Serving as efficient interfacial electron transmission bridges, the intercalated CDs establish dual charge transfer pathways to simultaneously accelerate the electron flow of Z-scheme CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction and the redox cycle of Cu2+/Cu+, leading to the synergetic photo-Fenton-like system.

Taking account of the aforementioned results, the possible photo-Fenton-like catalytic mechanism over CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 was proposed (Scheme 2 ). g-C3N4 and CuO are excited by visible light irradiation, generating photoinduced electron-hole pairs (e−-h+). Due to the more negative potential than E0 (O2/•O2 −) (−0.046 V vs. NHE) and E0(H2O2/•OH) (0.38 V vs. NHE), partial e− in the CB of g-C3N4 would react with O2 or H2O2 to generate reactive •O2 − or •OH radicals, respectively [50,51]. More importantly, more electrons accumulated in the CB of g-C3N4 can be efficiently transferred to accelerate the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ through interfacial electron transmission bridges of CDs. With the assistance of the CDs bridges, e− in the CB of CuO can also be rapidly transferred to recombine with h+ in the VB of g-C3N4, further strengthening the Z-scheme heterojunction. With higher oxidation capacity, more h+ in the VB of CuO can directly eliminate organic pollutants or react with OH− to produce •OH radicals (E0(OH−/•OH) = 1.99 V vs. NHE) [52]. In consequence, the dual charge transfer pathways can not only efficiently suppress the photoinduced e−-h+ recombination in the photocatalytic process, but also accelerate the transformation of Cu2+/Cu+ in the Fenton-like reaction. By rationally merging photocatalysis with Fenton-like catalysis, a synergetic CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 catalyst with superior catalytic performance is achieved by strengthening interfacial charge transfer, declaring great application potential for wastewater remediation.

In conclusion, a ternary CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid has been successfully developed as a superior visible-light-driven photo-Fenton-like catalyst for wastewater remediation. The coralloid flower-like g-C3N4 provides an ideal platform for hybridization with CuO and visible light harvesting. Serving as interfacial electron transmission bridges, the intercalated CDs construct dual charge transfer pathways between the Z-scheme CuO/g-C3N4 heterojunction interfaces, which can significantly enhance the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ in the Fenton-like cycle and accelerate the Z-scheme electron flow from CuO to g-C3N4, achieving the synergetic photo-Fenton-like system. Consequently, CuO/CDs/g-C3N4 hybrid showed remarkable catalytic efficiency towards recalcitrant organic pollutant degradation, excellent tolerance to realistic environmental conditions, exceptional stability, and wide universality, demonstrating great potential for practical applications. •O2 − and •OH free radicals are demonstrated as the primary contributors in photo-Fenton-like system. This work would afford a promising strategy to develop advanced photo-Fenton-like oxidation catalysts for solar energy-assisted water purification applications.
